frozen_components: context-sensitive parser (Tree of Parsers with modular CFGs)
architecture.summary: A constrained decoding system that pairs a language model with a context-sensitive parser (Tree of Parsers) to generate semantically correct programs. At each decoding step, the parser outputs a regex satisfying a non-extensibility property; this regex guides token generation via adaptive rejection sampling. The parser maintains a dynamic tree of modular context-free grammar nodes enriched with semantic context (variable scopes, type constraints); as tokens are consumed, parser branches are pruned to keep only valid continuation paths. A token healing procedure handles misalignment when LM tokens cross regex boundaries.

biblio.title: Correctness-Guaranteed Code Generation via Constrained Decoding

claims.statement: The context-sensitive parser outputs a regex at each step that satisfies the non-extensible match property (A1), which ensures the LM can confidently stop generation once the regex is matched and advance the parser state.

baseline.name: Willard & Louf (2023) + Koo et al. (2024) regex compilation

claims.dataset: DCI talent category generation (single example with 8 A100 GPUs, Qwen2.5-32B-Coder)
claims.statement: Adaptive rejection sampling achieves more than three times faster generation speed compared to alternatives (Willard & Louf 2023; Koo et al. 2024) when used in Algorithm 1.

claims.dataset: 8 talent category prompts, 10 runs each (80 data points total per method)
claims.statement: Our method with constrained decoding achieves the highest success rate and quality scores in generating DCI talent categories compared to unconstrained Claude-3.5-Sonnet and Qwen2.5-32B-Coder, with 33 data points in the highest quality range (90-100) versus Claude-3.5-Sonnet's 38 data points when both use reflection.
The paper's method failed only when generation did not terminate after the output token limit, typically leading to indefinite repetition due to constrained decoding distorting the LM distribution.
Algorithm 1 itself is not guaranteed to terminate; nontermination is a well-known issue in constrained decoding and part of the broader issue of distribution distortion.

For sLua with bounded variable count, nesting depth, and scope nesting, next_regex and has_finished functions have constant time complexity, and feed_text has linear time complexity in input size (Theorem 5.1).

Scripts successfully generated for talents and effects in DCI using Algorithm 1 are guaranteed to terminate and execute without runtime errors in the game engine, given the API design and language feature restrictions (Theorem 6.1).

The method can generate semantically correct programs conforming to any prescribed scripting API, with correctness defined by the parser's grammar and semantic constraints (variable scopes, type constraints).
